title: security(cli): change-password exposes passwords in shell history and process list
body:
## Summary

The `ofc account change-password` command accepts both `--current <password>` and `--new <password>` as CLI flags. This means passwords are:

- Stored verbatim in `~/.bash_history` / `~/.zsh_history`
- Visible to any user who runs `ps aux` or reads `/proc/<pid>/cmdline` while the command is running
- Captured by CI/CD log systems if used in scripts

## Affected File

`cli/ofc.ts` — `account change-password` command (lines 303–320)

```ts
.requiredOption('--current <password>', 'Current password')
.requiredOption('--new <password>', 'New password')
```

## Recommended Fix

Replace `--current` / `--new` option flags with interactive `promptPassword()` calls (the same pattern used by `auth login`), and add a `--password-stdin` option for scripted use. No passwords should be accepted as positional arguments or named options.

## Severity
🔴 **Critical** — silently leaks credentials on every use today.

title: security(auth): no per-account brute-force / login-attempt protection
body:
## Summary

The global `authLimiter` (20 requests / 15 min per IP) is the **only** protection against credential guessing. A distributed attack using one guess per IP can exhaust the entire password space of low-entropy accounts without triggering any limit. There is no per-account failed-login counter, no lockout, no progressive delay, and no alert.

## Affected File

`src/api/com.atproto.server.createSession.ts`

## Recommended Fix

1. Add `failed_login_attempts` and `locked_until` columns to the `users` table.
2. Increment the counter on each failed authentication attempt for the matched account.
3. After 5 consecutive failures apply an exponential back-off lockout (e.g., 1 min → 5 min → 30 min).
4. Emit an audit log entry for each failed login including the IP address.
5. Reset the counter on successful login.

## Severity
🔴 **Critical** — no mitigations exist beyond per-IP rate limiting.

title: security(activitypub): RSA keypair seed is computed but never applied — key is random per restart
body:
## Summary

In `src/activitypub/ap-routes.ts`, a deterministic seed is derived from the community DID and `KEY_ENCRYPTION_SECRET` but is **never passed** to `crypto.generateKeyPairSync` (which has no seed parameter). As a result:

1. Each server restart generates a **completely different** RSA keypair for every community DID.
2. In a multi-instance deployment, different instances have **different keys** for the same community.
3. ActivityPub HTTP Signatures are repudiated on every restart, breaking federation with Mastodon / GoToSocial.

## Affected File

`src/activitypub/ap-routes.ts` (lines 28–45)

```ts
const seed = crypto.createHash('sha256')
  .update(`ap-rsa-${did}-${config.keyEncryptionSecret}`)
  .digest('hex');

// seed is never used — generateKeyPairSync ignores it
const { publicKey } = crypto.generateKeyPairSync('rsa', { ... });
```

## Recommended Fix

Store RSA keypairs in the database (linked to the community DID), generating them once on first use and persisting them so they survive restarts and are consistent across instances. Encrypt the private key at rest using the existing `encryptKeyBytes` helper.

## Severity
🟠 **High** — breaks ActivityPub federation on every restart.

title: security(auth): users cannot view or revoke their own active sessions
body:
## Summary

A user who suspects account compromise has no way to:
- See which sessions are currently active (device, last used, IP)
- Revoke individual sessions they don't recognise

The database has all the required data (`sessions` table with `id`, `user_id`, `expires_at`, `last_used_at`) — the API endpoints simply don't exist.

The only current option is `deleteSession` which logs out the *current* session only. Admins similarly cannot view or revoke a specific user's sessions without manually querying the database.

## Recommended New Endpoints

- `net.openfederation.account.listSessions` — returns list of user's active sessions (self or admin view)
- `net.openfederation.account.revokeSession` — revokes a specific session by ID (self or admin)

## Recommended CLI Commands

```
ofc account sessions list
ofc account sessions revoke <session-id>
ofc account sessions revoke-all
ofc admin account sessions list <handle>
ofc admin account sessions revoke-all <handle>
```

## Severity
🟠 **High** — no incident response capability for compromised accounts.

title: security(auth): no self-service password reset mechanism
body:
## Summary

There is no password reset / forgot-password flow. A user who loses their password must contact the PDS administrator and request a manual reset. This:
- Puts the entire burden of identity verification on the admin
- Creates a **social engineering attack surface**: an attacker can impersonate a legitimate user, contact the admin, and request a password reset
- Is a poor user experience with no recovery path

## Recommended Fix

Implement an email-based reset flow:
1. User submits their email/handle to a `POST /xrpc/net.openfederation.account.requestPasswordReset` endpoint
2. A time-limited (e.g., 1 hour), single-use reset token is generated and emailed
3. User submits the token + new password to `POST /xrpc/net.openfederation.account.confirmPasswordReset`
4. Success: password updated, all sessions revoked, audit log entry written

Also add a CLI command for admins to trigger a reset email on behalf of a user:
```
ofc admin account reset-password <handle>
```
And an identity verification challenge (sign a nonce with the DID signing key) to allow admins to verify identity before manual reset.

## Severity
🟠 **High** — no secure account recovery path; social engineering risk.

title: security(crypto): pbkdf2Sync blocks the Node.js event loop during key encryption/decryption
security(oauth): in-memory pending-code Map has no size cap (memory DoS)
security(server): no Content Security Policy (CSP) header set
body:
## Summary

The server sets several security headers (`X-Frame-Options`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, etc.) but does **not** set a `Content-Security-Policy` header. The server renders HTML at `/` and serves `/sdk/v1.js`. Without CSP, any stored/reflected user-controlled content in a browser context has no XSS mitigation fallback.

## Affected File

`src/server/index.ts` — security headers middleware (lines 117–126)

## Recommended Fix

Add to the security headers middleware:

```ts
res.setHeader(
  'Content-Security-Policy',
  "default-src 'none'; script-src 'self'; connect-src 'self'; " +
  "img-src 'self' data:; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; frame-ancestors 'none'"
);
```

This can be adjusted based on what the web UI and SDK actually need.

## Severity
🟡 **Medium** — missing defence-in-depth layer against XSS.

title: security(audit): failed login attempts are not written to the audit log
body:
## Summary

Every successful login is trackable (a session row is created), but **failed authentication attempts are not recorded** in the audit log. This makes it impossible to detect brute-force attacks, credential stuffing campaigns, or compromised accounts after the fact by reviewing audit records.

## Affected File

`src/api/com.atproto.server.createSession.ts`

## Recommended Fix

Add an audit log entry on each failed authentication attempt:

```ts
await auditLog('session.loginFailed', null, user?.id ?? null, {
  identifier: input.identifier,
  reason: 'wrong_password',
  ip: req.ip,
});
```

Also extend the `AuditAction` type in `src/db/audit.ts` to include `'session.loginFailed'`.

## Severity
🟡 **Medium** — prevents post-incident forensic analysis.

title: security(federation): peer health checks lack SSRF guard
security(api): attestation claim field has no size or depth limit
security(api): invite.create has no per-issuer rate limit
body:
## Summary

Any admin or moderator can call `net.openfederation.invite.create` with no throttling. A compromised moderator account can generate unlimited invite codes and distribute them, enabling mass-registration attacks that bypass `INVITE_REQUIRED`.

## Affected File

`src/api/net.openfederation.invite.create.ts` — no rate limiter in route registration (`src/server/index.ts`)

## Recommended Fix

**Option A** — Apply the existing `createLimiter` to the route:
```ts
'net.openfederation.invite.create': { handler: createInvite, limiter: createLimiter },
```

**Option B** — Add a max-active-invites check per issuer (e.g., max 50 unused invite codes at a time per moderator).

Also write an audit entry for every invite creation that includes the code's expiry and max-uses, so bulk creation is visible in the audit log.

## Severity
🟡 **Medium** — invite-gated registration can be bypassed by a compromised moderator.

title: docs(deployment): trust proxy level needs environment-specific documentation
body:
## Summary

`src/server/index.ts` sets `app.set('trust proxy', 1)` which trusts exactly one proxy hop. Deployments with Cloudflare + Railway have **two proxy hops**, meaning the rate limiter may see Cloudflare's anycast IP rather than the real client IP — defeating per-IP rate limiting entirely.

## Recommended Changes

1. Add an `EXPRESS_TRUST_PROXY` environment variable (default: `1`) that maps to Express's trust-proxy setting.
2. Document the correct value for each supported deployment topology in `DEPLOYMENT.md`:
   - Direct / VPS: `1`
   - Railway only: `1`
   - Cloudflare + Railway: `2` (or `true` to trust all)
3. Add to `.env.example` with a comment.

## Severity
🟡 **Medium** — incorrect trust-proxy silently breaks IP-based rate limiting.

title: security(social): PLC rotation key — no warning that PDS will never request it again
body:
## Summary

When a community or user DID is created, a **primary rotation key** is shown exactly once. There is no explicit warning that **the PDS will never request this key** and that any request for it is a phishing attempt.

**Attack scenario:** An attacker sends a message to a community creator: *"Your rotation key has been flagged as compromised. Please submit it to security@openfederation.net for re-issuance."* Users who kept the key may send it, granting the attacker full control over the DID.

## Recommended Fix

1. Update the key-creation message and CLI output to explicitly state: *"OpenFederation and PDS operators will NEVER ask you for this key. Any request for it is a phishing attempt."*
2. Add a **"What We Will Never Ask"** section to `SECURITY.md` covering rotation keys, passwords, and session tokens.
3. Include the same warning text in the web UI's key-display component.

## Severity
🔴 **Critical (social)** — total DID takeover if key is phished.

title: security(social): admin password-reset has no identity verification protocol
body:
## Summary

There is no self-service password reset. Users must contact the PDS admin to recover access. However, there is **no defined identity verification protocol** for admins to follow before resetting a password.

**Attack scenario:** An attacker impersonates a legitimate user, emails the PDS admin: *"Hi, I'm alice, I've lost access to my account. Can you reset my password?"* The admin cannot verify identity from an email alone, so may comply, handing the account to the attacker.

## Recommended Fix

1. Define an **identity verification protocol** in `DEPLOYMENT.md`: admin must require the user to prove DID control by signing a server-issued nonce with their signing key.
2. Add a CLI command: `ofc admin account verify <handle>` — issues a nonce challenge that the user must sign.
3. Document that admins should **never** reset a password based solely on an email request.

## Severity
🟠 **High (social)** — account takeover via social engineering of the admin.

title: security(social): reserved handle list too narrow — handle squatting enables impersonation
body:
## Summary

The reserved handles list in `src/auth/utils.ts` covers obvious names (`admin`, `moderator`, `support`) but misses many common trust-signal handles. An attacker can register `security`, `official`, `staff`, `team`, `legal`, or `privacy` and send messages appearing to represent the platform.

**Attack scenario:** Attacker registers `security` on a PDS and DMs members: *"Hello, I'm from the OpenFederation security team. Please share your current session token so we can investigate."*

## Recommended Fix

Expand `RESERVED_HANDLES` in `src/auth/utils.ts`:

```ts
'official', 'security', 'staff', 'team', 'ops', 'operations',
'abuse', 'contact', 'info', 'news', 'legal', 'privacy', 'tos',
'trust', 'safety', 'bot', 'service', 'noreply', 'no-reply',
```

Also consider a `verified_operator` role that is visually distinguished in the UI.

## Severity
🟠 **High (social)** — platform impersonation enabling credential theft.

title: security(social): community transfer needs time-delay and owner email notification
body:
## Summary

`net.openfederation.community.transfer` transfers community ownership immediately with no confirmation delay, email notification to the current owner, or re-authentication requirement.

If a community owner's session is stolen, a community transfer is instant and irreversible.

## Recommended Fix

1. **Require re-authentication** (current password confirmation) to initiate a transfer.
2. **Introduce a 48-hour hold period** before the transfer takes effect — send the current owner an email with a cancellation link.
3. **Add CLI confirmation requirement**:
```
ofc community transfer <did> --new-owner <did> --confirm
```
4. Emit a high-severity audit log entry so monitors can alert on transfers.

## Severity
🟠 **High (social)** — irreversible community takeover via stolen session.

title: security(social): fake partner key request — no domain verification required
body:
## Summary

Partner keys bypass invite requirements and auto-approve users. However, there is **no domain verification or vetting process** defined. A malicious actor can register a plausible-sounding app and request a partner key via email.

**Attack scenario:** Attacker creates a GitHub page for "OpenFederation Mobile Client", emails the PDS operator requesting a partner key. Key is granted. The attacker's app auto-creates unlimited approved accounts for spam/abuse.

## Recommended Fix

1. Require a `domain` field when requesting a partner key; verify domain ownership via DNS TXT record challenge.
2. Set a **low default rate limit** for new partner keys (e.g., 10 registrations/hour) requiring an explicit increase request.
3. Make `allowed_origins` required (not optional) for all new partner keys.
4. Document the vetting process in `DEPLOYMENT.md`.

## Severity
🟠 **High (social)** — partner keys enable mass account creation bypassing invite-gating.

title: security(social): bootstrap admin password env var not cleared after initial setup
body:
## Summary

`BOOTSTRAP_ADMIN_PASSWORD` is set in `.env` and read at every server startup. Many operators leave this set indefinitely — meaning it is readable by anyone with filesystem access and will appear in environment variable dumps.

The current code correctly skips re-creating the admin if it already exists, but provides **no warning** that the variable should be removed after first use.

## Recommended Fix

1. After detecting the bootstrap admin already exists in the database, print a **startup warning** if `BOOTSTRAP_ADMIN_PASSWORD` is still set:
```
WARNING: BOOTSTRAP_ADMIN_PASSWORD is still set in your environment.
The admin account is already created — remove this variable now to reduce your attack surface.
```
2. Update `.env.example` comment to say: *"Remove this variable after first startup. Keeping it set is a security risk."*
3. Consider supporting `BOOTSTRAP_ADMIN_PASSWORD_FILE` (Docker secrets pattern).

## Severity
🟡 **Medium (social)** — credential exposure via environment variable persistence.

title: security(social): ATProto OAuth redirect does not show the target PDS URL before redirecting
body:
## Summary

The external ATProto login flow (`GET /auth/atproto?handle=...`) silently redirects users to their home PDS for consent.

**Attack scenario:** Attacker registers a convincing domain as a did:web PDS, then sends phishing links: *"Your account needs re-verification. Click here to log in: https://realpds.example.com/auth/atproto?handle=victim@malicious-pds.com"*. The user is silently sent to the attacker's fake PDS login page.

## Recommended Fix

1. **Before redirect**, render an intermediate confirmation page showing the full PDS URL prominently: *"You will be redirected to `https://malicious-pds.com` to authenticate. Verify this is your home PDS before continuing."*
2. Add a **delay + explicit "Continue" button** for first-time or unfamiliar PDS domains.
3. Warn visually if the handle domain doesn't match the resolved PDS service endpoint domain.

## Severity
🟡 **Medium (social)** — transparent credential phishing via external PDS redirect.

title: cli: add ofc account sessions list/revoke commands
body:
## Summary

Users currently have no way to see or manage their active sessions from the CLI. The `ofc auth logout` command only logs out the current session. If a device is lost or a session is suspected to be compromised, the only option is to change the password.

## Proposed Commands

```
ofc account sessions list
```
Lists all active sessions for the current user: ID prefix, created, last used, expires.

```
ofc account sessions revoke <session-id>
```
Revokes a specific session by its ID.

```
ofc account sessions revoke-all
```
Revokes all sessions except the current one.

**Admin variants:**
```
ofc admin account sessions list <handle>
ofc admin account sessions revoke-all <handle>
```

## Required API Changes

New XRPC endpoints needed:
- `net.openfederation.account.listSessions`
- `net.openfederation.account.revokeSession`

title: cli: add ofc security check-config command
body:
## Summary

Add a `ofc security check-config` command that validates the PDS server configuration for common security misconfigurations.

## Proposed Output

```
$ ofc security check-config
✔ AUTH_JWT_SECRET: strong (64 chars)
✔ KEY_ENCRYPTION_SECRET: set
⚠ BOOTSTRAP_ADMIN_PASSWORD: still set — remove after first startup
✘ DB_SSL: false — enable SSL for production databases
⚠ REDIS_URL: not set — OAuth replay protection is in-memory only
✔ NODE_ENV: production
✔ INVITE_REQUIRED: true
```

## Checks to Implement

- `AUTH_JWT_SECRET` >= 32 chars and not a known default
- `KEY_ENCRYPTION_SECRET` is set
- `OAUTH_SIGNING_KEY` is set (not auto-generated) in production
- `BOOTSTRAP_ADMIN_PASSWORD` cleared after initial setup
- `DB_SSL=true` in production
- `NODE_ENV=production` is set
- Redis configured (not in-memory OAuth token store)
- `INVITE_REQUIRED=true`
- `ACCESS_TOKEN_TTL` <= 15m

title: cli: add ofc security audit-summary command
body:
## Summary

Add `ofc security audit-summary [--days <n>]` to provide a quick overview of recent security events from the audit log.

## Proposed Output

```
$ ofc security audit-summary --days 7
Security summary for the last 7 days:

  Failed logins:              142  (up from 38 last week)
  Token reuse detections:       1
  Admin role changes:           2
  Partner key events:           1 (1 created, 0 revoked)
  Community transfers:          0
  Password changes:             8
  Account takedowns:            0
```

## Events to Summarise

- `session.loginFailed` (once implemented)
- Refresh token reuse detections (add to audit log)
- `account.roles.update`
- `partner.key.create` / `partner.key.revoke`
- `community.transfer.initiate`
- `account.password.change`
- `account.takedown` / `account.suspend`

title: cli: ofc community transfer should require --confirm flag like community delete
body:
## Summary

`ofc community delete` correctly requires a `--confirm <handle>` flag before proceeding. However, `ofc community transfer <did> --new-owner <did>` has **no confirmation step at all** — it executes the transfer immediately.

Community transfer is equally irreversible: once transferred, the original owner loses all access to their community.

## Proposed Behaviour

```
$ ofc community transfer did:plc:abc123 --new-owner did:plc:xyz789
This will permanently transfer ownership of community did:plc:abc123 to did:plc:xyz789.
You will lose all owner privileges. This cannot be undone.
Pass --confirm to proceed.

$ ofc community transfer did:plc:abc123 --new-owner did:plc:xyz789 --confirm
Community transferred to did:plc:xyz789
```

## Affected File

`cli/ofc.ts` — `community transfer` command

title: cli: ofc invite create should support --bound-to <email> option
body:
## Summary

Invite codes are shared out-of-band (email, Slack, DM). Currently any recipient of an invite code can use it — there is no way to bind a code to a specific recipient. This means:
1. Intercepted invite codes can be used by anyone
2. Moderators have no record of who an invite was intended for

## Proposed Change

```
ofc invite create --bound-to alice@example.com --note "Invited by Bob from team meeting"
```

When `boundTo` is set, the registration endpoint verifies that the registering email matches (case-insensitive) before accepting the invite code.

## API Changes Required

- `net.openfederation.invite.create`: add optional `boundTo` (email) and `note` (string) fields
- `net.openfederation.account.register`: validate `boundTo` match when set on the invite record
- `invites` table: add `bound_to` (email, nullable) and `note` (text, nullable) columns
